Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a routine. I'd tell myself I'd study on certain days, but then I just... wouldn't. Sometimes I'd sit down with my laptop intending to work, but I'd end up scrolling or just staring at the screen feeling overwhelmed. With 17 courses to juggle, everything felt chaotic and I never figured out a system that actually worked for me.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. I'd go days without touching anything, then suddenly panic when a deadline was close and try to cram everything in. It wasn't intentional—life stuff would come up, or I'd just feel too sad to focus. I kept meaning to create a regular schedule but it never stuck.
Q3: Usually it was panic about a deadline or getting an email reminder that something was due. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if I'd missed anything important, which I often had. It wasn't like I wanted to engage—it felt more like damage control most of the time.
Q4: Not really, no. There were maybe a week or two where I logged in more regularly, but it never became automatic for me. I think I needed more structure or accountability to make it a habit, and I just couldn't create that for myself. The LMS always felt like this overwhelming thing I had to face rather than something I naturally went to.
Q5: The videos were probably the most useful because I learn better when I can see and hear things being explained. I watched quite a few hours of them, actually. Reading dense text on a screen doesn't work for me—I need something more active. But even the videos felt hard to get through when I was in a low mood.
Q6: I ignored the discussion boards a lot. Posting felt awkward and I didn't see the point when I was already behind on everything else. The reading materials too—I'd open them but not actually read them properly. It felt like there was always something more urgent, so the optional stuff just fell away.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see how it directly connected to passing or to something I'd actually need to know. If it felt abstract or like busy work, I couldn't motivate myself. I needed to feel like my effort would actually matter, which was hard when I was already doing poorly.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford. When you're already drowning in required stuff you haven't done, optional things feel almost insulting—like, who has time for that? Required ones at least had the threat of consequences, which was sometimes the only thing that got me moving.
Q9: Yeah, I'd download PDFs and videos to my laptop so I could study without internet sometimes, since my connection isn't always reliable. I also used my phone to look up explanations on YouTube when the course materials didn't make sense. A lot of my actual learning happened away from the LMS.
Q10: There were nights where I'd sit with printed notes or downloaded slides, trying to understand concepts by working through them by hand. I'm a kinesthetic learner—I need to write things out, move around, do something physical. None of that shows up anywhere in the system, but it was real studying.
Q11: Definitely. I'd watch a video once online, then download it and rewatch it later when I was trying to actually understand it. Or I'd screenshot slides and study from those on my phone during commutes. The LMS only saw that first click, not all the time I spent with the material afterward.
Q12: Around midterms everything fell apart for me. I was feeling really sad and unmotivated, and the workload from all my courses just piled up. I'd open the LMS and feel this wave of anxiety seeing all the things I hadn't done. It became easier to just... not look at it.
Q13: Absolutely. Being tired and sad made everything harder. When you're already feeling low, logging into a system that shows you all your failures is the last thing you want to do. I'd avoid it for days, which made things worse, which made me feel worse. It was a bad cycle.
Q14: So many times. I'd scroll through materials or have a video playing but my mind was completely elsewhere. I was going through the motions without actually absorbing anything. It looked like engagement from the outside, but inside I was just... empty. Present but not really there.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even when I knew they were important. Part of it was feeling overwhelmed and not knowing where to start. Part of it was this weird avoidance thing where looking at the work made me anxious, so I'd put it off to avoid that feeling. I know it's self-defeating but I couldn't stop.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that made me do anything. I'd ignore stuff until the last possible moment, then scramble to submit something—anything. It wasn't good work, but at least it was something. Without deadlines, I probably wouldn't have submitted anything at all.
Q17: When I actually got feedback, it sometimes motivated me briefly. But often the feedback came when I'd already moved on mentally, or it confirmed what I already knew—that I was struggling. It's hard to feel encouraged when you're already feeling like a failure. The mindfulness stuff they suggested helped a tiny bit with the anxiety though.
Q18: The structure felt overwhelming with 17 courses. There was too much to keep track of, and I couldn't figure out what to prioritize. If things had been more streamlined or if there were clearer pathways, maybe I could have managed better. Instead it felt like drowning in options and obligations.
Q19: The lack of clear guidance on what was most important made things harder. Everything seemed equally urgent, which meant nothing felt manageable. Also, the platform itself felt cold and impersonal—just lists of tasks and deadlines without any human connection. That made it easier to disengage.
Q20: I think that was me sometimes. You log in, click around, maybe watch some videos or read some stuff, but when it comes to actually producing work, you freeze. The consuming part is passive and feels safer. Creating and submitting work means being judged, and when you're already feeling bad about yourself, that's terrifying.
Q21: I guess some people are just more efficient or they study in ways that don't show up online. For me, my low activity matched my low performance, so I can't claim that excuse. But I can see how someone who learns well from textbooks or study groups wouldn't need the LMS much.
Q22: A course log can show when you clicked on things and for how long, but it can't show if you actually understood anything or if you were just staring blankly at the screen. It misses all the emotional stuff—the sadness, the anxiety, the feeling of being lost. It's just numbers without context.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and feeling like you're part of something. It means showing up mentally, not just physically clicking buttons. For me, real engagement would mean feeling curious and motivated, not just anxious and obligated. I didn't feel that this semester.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how sad I've been, or how hard it is to focus when you're feeling low. It wouldn't show the hours I spent staring at my ceiling trying to find motivation, or the guilt I felt every time I avoided logging in. It sees a disengaged student; it doesn't see someone who's struggling just to get through each day.
